summary: I grew up in Tulsa, Oklahoma, where I attended my first school, Longfellow. After a brief stay in Portland, Oregon, we returned to Tulsa and I continued my education there at Whittier School, Grover Cleveland Junior High, and finally Will Rogers High School, graduating in 1953. Tulsa was home for most of my childhood and teenage years, and many of my early memories, including living near Archer Park and visiting downtown stores, are rooted there.

The first school I attended was Longfellow School in Tulsa, Oklahoma. It was located at 6th and Peoria, but the original building is gone now and replaced by a new one. I was there during my Pre-Prime Harry and Kindergarten years before we moved briefly to Portland, Oregon.

My family and I lived in Portland, Oregon for about a year during the early 1940s. My dad had volunteered for the army but was turned down because of polio, so he found work in the shipyards along the Willamette River. We stayed there until he secured a house back in Tulsa, after which we returned home.

While living in Portland, Oregon, my dad worked in the shipyards located on the Willamette River. This was during the time when he was unable to serve in the military due to his polio-affected leg. The shipyards provided employment for him during the war period before we moved back to Tulsa.

After returning from Portland, I was enrolled at Whittier School in Tulsa, where I attended from third through sixth grade. We were living with an aunt near Zunis and 3rd Street at the time. Whittier was an important part of my early education before I moved on to junior high.

Following my years at Whittier School, I attended Grover Cleveland Junior High School in Tulsa. This was the next step in my education before I went on to high school at Will Rogers. It was part of my schooling during the late 1940s.

I graduated from Will Rogers High School in Tulsa in 1953. It was the final stage of my formal education before law school. Attending Will Rogers was a significant part of my youth, and it followed my time at Grover Cleveland Junior High.

Archer Park was right across the street from the house we bought in 1944 at 2854 East Archer. The park was central to much of our entertainment as kids, with swings and organized games during the summer. The city hired men and women to supervise and keep us busy, and I remember a man named John who was very active in organizing activities there.

The Circle Theatre was a key place for entertainment in my childhood. I loved going there to watch movies, especially Hopalong Cassidy, who was my hero. The theater was part of a small building that also housed Doc's Candy Store, and sometimes our parents would take us to see films there.

Hopalong Cassidy was my hero growing up. Watching his movies at the Circle Theatre was one of the best things we could do for fun. There was nothing better than seeing Hopalong on the big screen, and it was a highlight of my childhood entertainment.

Doc's Candy Store was part of the same building as the Circle Theatre in Tulsa. After watching movies like Hopalong Cassidy, we would often go into Doc's to get candy. I think the building might still be there, and it was a memorable part of our entertainment routine.

Mr. Griffin's Hamburger was a small hamburger stand located at Braden and Anvil in Tulsa. It was a narrow building with a counter and stools, no air conditioning, just a ventilator fan. Dad would sometimes take us there for hamburgers and Pepsi, and the smell of onions cooking on the grill was unforgettable—it was a real treat for us back then.

Braden and Anvil was the intersection where Mr. Griffin's Hamburger stand was located. It was a place I remember well from my childhood in Tulsa because Dad would take us there for hamburgers. The stand was a small, narrow building with a counter and stools, and it was a special outing for us.

Pepsi Cola was the drink we often had when visiting Mr. Griffin's Hamburger stand. It was part of the simple pleasures of my childhood in Tulsa, enjoying a cold Pepsi with a freshly grilled hamburger. That was considered high life for us at the time.

Brown-Duncan was one of the downtown stores in Tulsa that I remember well. My mother even worked there one or two Christmas seasons selling Christmas cards. We visited downtown quite a bit, and Brown-Duncan was among the active stores alongside others like Vandevers and Seidenbach's.

Vandevers was one of the prominent stores downtown in Tulsa during my youth. Along with Brown-Duncan, Seidenbach's, Froug's, and Field's, it was part of the shopping scene we experienced growing up. These stores were familiar places for us in the city.

Seidenbach's was another downtown Tulsa store I remember from my childhood. It was one of several well-known stores like Brown-Duncan and Vandevers where people shopped. These stores were part of the city's commercial life in those days.

Froug's was among the downtown stores in Tulsa that I recall from growing up. It was part of the retail environment alongside other stores like Brown-Duncan and Seidenbach's. We visited these stores often as part of our city life.

Field's was one of the stores downtown in Tulsa that I remember from my youth. It was part of the group of stores including Brown-Duncan and Froug's where people shopped. These stores were a familiar part of the city during my childhood.

I can't quite recall if Penny's had a downtown outlet in Tulsa during my childhood, but it was mentioned among the stores we knew about. The downtown shopping scene included places like Brown-Duncan and Seidenbach's, and Penny's might have been part of that environment.

I had an uncle who joined the United States Marine Corps around the time of World War II. While I was too young to remember the announcement of the war, I recall that my uncle served in the Marines during that period. His service was part of the larger context of the war impacting our family.

I was about seven years old when the attack on Pearl Harbor happened on December 7, 1941. I don't remember the announcement itself, but I do remember the events that followed and how it affected my family, including my uncle who joined the Marine Corps. That event marked the beginning of a significant time in our lives.
